rawHead:
<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="utf-8" />
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1" />
<title>ZFC and ordinals — axioms, transfinite numbers, and the continuum</title>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.css">
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.js"></script>
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/contrib/auto-render.min.js"></script>
<script>
(function(){
  function renderAll(){
    if(typeof renderMathInElement!=='function'){ return setTimeout(renderAll,50); }
    renderMathInElement(document.body,{
      delimiters:[
        {left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},
        {left:'$',right:'$',display:false},
        {left:'\\(',right:'\\)',display:false},
        {left:'\\[',right:'\\]',display:true}
      ],
      throwOnError:false
    });
  }
  if(document.readyState==='loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded',renderAll);
  else renderAll();
})();
</script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:var(--blue)}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:var(--yellow)}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;
    padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select{
    background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .7rem;cursor:pointer;font:inherit}
  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  button.active{background:var(--yellow);color:#111;border-color:var(--yellow)}
  .readout{background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;
    padding:.6rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;color:#cbd5e1;
    min-height:1.6em;white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word}
  .pill{display:inline-block;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:999px;padding:1px 8px;font-size:.82rem;color:var(--mute);margin-right:4px}
  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .ok{border-left:3px solid var(--green);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d5ebc8;background:#1a2418;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .bad{border-left:3px solid var(--pink);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#f4d2c7;background:#2a1a18;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%}
  svg text{fill:var(--ink);font-family:inherit}
  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);
    border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding:.6rem 1rem;z-index:10;
    display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}
  /* sticky sidebar TOC (wide screens only; narrow screens fall back to top-nav) */
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;
    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;
    padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);
    font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute);margin:0 0 .5rem;padding-bottom:.35rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc li{margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc a{display:block;padding:.3rem .5rem;color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none;
    border-left:2px solid transparent;line-height:1.35;
    transition:color 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease}
  aside.sidetoc a:hover{color:var(--ink)}
  aside.sidetoc a.active{color:var(--yellow);border-left-color:var(--yellow)}
  @media (max-width:1180px){ aside.sidetoc{display:none} }
  @media (min-width:1181px){ nav.toc a[href^="#"]{display:none} }
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem}
  .small{font-size:.88rem;color:var(--mute)}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}
  table.plain{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:.95rem;margin:.6rem 0}
  table.plain th,table.plain td{padding:.35rem .5rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);text-align:left}
  table.plain th{color:var(--mute);font-weight:500}
  /* callback-css-auto-begin */
  aside.callback{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:rgba(88,196,221,0.05);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--cyan) 45%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.callback .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.callback ul{margin:0;padding-left:1.1rem}
  aside.callback ul li{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.callback a{color:inherit}
/* callback-css-auto-end */
  details.changelog{
    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;
  }
  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);
  }
  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}
  details.changelog td{padding:.2rem .6rem;border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.07);vertical-align:top}
  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}
  aside.related{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-begin */
  html[data-hide-widgets] .widget { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-quizzes] .quiz { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-lineage] .lineage-strip { display: none !important; }
  .mv-display-toggles { display: inline-flex; gap: .6rem; align-items: center; }
  .mv-display-toggle { background: transparent; border: 1px solid var(--line, #333); border-radius: 6px; padding: 5px 10px; cursor: pointer; font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1; color: inherit; transition: background 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease; }
  .mv-display-toggle:hover { background: var(--panel, rgba(255,255,255,0.06)); border-color: var(--mute, #666); }
  .mv-display-toggle--off { opacity: 0.35; }
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-end */
</style>
<!-- breadcrumb-head-auto-begin -->

rawBodyPrefix:
← Notebook
1 ZFC axioms 2 Von Neumann ordinals 3 Ordinal arithmetic 4 Cardinals & cofinality 5 Axiom of choice 6 Continuum hypothesis 7 Inaccessibles

ZFC and ordinals

Cantor's naive set theory, made bulletproof. Nine axioms tell you which collections deserve to be sets; once that's settled, every infinite size — countable or not — is named by a von Neumann ordinal, and the continuum hypothesis turns out to be a question the axioms simply do not answer.

Naive set theory has a leak: unrestricted comprehension $\{x : \varphi(x)\}$ admits $R = \{x : x\notin x\}$, and $R\in R \iff R\notin R$ (Russell, 1901). The fix, due to Zermelo (1908) and Fraenkel/Skolem (1922), is to declare a short list of set-construction axioms and forbid everything else. Add the axiom of choice and you have ZFC, the working foundation of essentially all modern mathematics.

This page does three things. First, it lists the nine axioms and points at what each one buys you. Second, it builds the von Neumann ordinals — a canonical set-theoretic name for every well-ordered length, finite or transfinite — and shows their non-commutative arithmetic. Third, it does cardinals, the axiom of choice, the continuum hypothesis, and a glance at large cardinals. The widgets are mostly diagrams: ordinals are visual objects, and once you've seen $\omega^2$ as a tower of $\omega$-pickets you remember it.

blocks.html:
1. The ZFC axioms

Everything below is a first-order sentence in the language $\{\in\}$. The axioms divide into three groups: identity (extensionality, regularity), construction (pairing, union, power set, infinity), and comprehension (separation, replacement, choice).

axiomsaysbuys you
Extensionality$\forall x\forall y\,[\forall z(z\in x\leftrightarrow z\in y)\to x=y]$sets are determined by their elements
Pairing$\forall a\forall b\,\exists p\,(p=\{a,b\})$$\{a\}$, $\{a,b\}$, ordered pair $(a,b)=\{\{a\},\{a,b\}\}$
Union$\forall F\,\exists U\,\forall x(x\in U \leftrightarrow \exists A\in F\,x\in A)$$\bigcup F$ for any family $F$
Power set$\forall A\,\exists P\,\forall S(S\in P \leftrightarrow S\subseteq A)$$\mathcal P(A)$, hence function spaces, $\mathbb R$
Infinity$\exists I\,(\emptyset\in I \wedge \forall x\in I\,x\cup\{x\}\in I)$$\omega$, hence $\mathbb N$
Separation (schema)$\forall A\,\exists B\,\forall x(x\in B\leftrightarrow x\in A\wedge\varphi(x))$safe comprehension; blocks Russell
Replacement (schema)image of a set under a definable functional class is a set$V_\alpha$ for transfinite $\alpha$, recursion
Regularity$\forall A\ne\emptyset\,\exists x\in A\,(x\cap A=\emptyset)$no $x\in x$, well-foundedness, ranks
Choiceevery family of nonempty sets has a choice functionZorn, well-ordering, Tychonoff
Why Separation, not Comprehension. The naive axiom $\exists B\,\forall x(x\in B\leftrightarrow\varphi(x))$ — for arbitrary $\varphi$ — gives Russell's paradox by taking $\varphi(x)\equiv x\notin x$. Separation requires an ambient set $A$, so $\{x\in A: x\notin x\}$ exists harmlessly (it is a subset of $A$), but the universal $R=\{x:x\notin x\}$ is never formed.

The cumulative hierarchy $V$ is built by transfinite recursion on the ordinals: $V_0=\emptyset$, $V_{\alpha+1}=\mathcal P(V_\alpha)$, $V_\lambda=\bigcup_{\alpha<\lambda}V_\alpha$ at limits. Every set lies in some $V_\alpha$ (this is what regularity ensures), and the universe is the proper class $V=\bigcup_\alpha V_\alpha$. Click the rank slider:

Cumulative hierarchy $V_\alpha$
drag to climb the ranks

See also
  • Cartesian products and power sets
  • Sets and functions

<script>
(function(){
  const slider = $('#cum-r'), lab = $('#cum-lab'), svg = $('#cum-svg'), out = $('#cum-out');
  // |V_n| for n=0..6 is 0,1,2,4,16,65536,2^65536 — the last is astronomical
  const sizes = [0,1,2,4,16,65536,Number.POSITIVE_INFINITY];
  const labels = ['V_0 = ∅', 'V_1 = {∅}', 'V_2 = {∅, {∅}}', 'V_3', 'V_4', 'V_5', 'V_ω (limit)'];
  function render(){
    const r = parseInt(slider.value,10);
    svg.innerHTML='';
    lab.textContent = `α = ${r===6?'ω':r}`;
    const cx = 310, cy = 250;
    // draw nested rings
    for(let k=0;k<=r;k++){
      const radius = 30 + k*22;
      const ring = SVG('circle',{cx, cy, r:radius, fill:'none',
        stroke: k===r? 'var(--yellow)' : 'var(--line)',
        'stroke-width': k===r? 2 : 1,
        'stroke-dasharray': k===r? null : '3 3'});
      svg.appendChild(ring);
      const lbl = SVG('text',{x:cx, y:cy-radius-4,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':11,
        fill: k===r? 'var(--yellow)' : 'var(--mute)'});
      lbl.textContent = `V_${k===6?'ω':k}`;
      svg.appendChild(lbl);
    }
    // central dot = ∅
    svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx, cy, r:5, fill:'var(--violet)'}));
    const t = SVG('text',{x:cx+10, y:cy+4,'font-size':12,fill:'var(--mute)'});
    t.textContent='∅'; svg.appendChild(t);
    // size readout
    const sz = sizes[r];
    const szStr = (r===6) ? 'ℵ₀ (already infinite)'
                : (r===5) ? '2^16 = 65,536'
                : String(sz);
    out.textContent =
`current rank      = V_${r===6?'ω':r}
description       = ${labels[r]}
|V_${r===6?'ω':r}|             = ${szStr}
construction rule = V_{α+1} = P(V_α);   V_λ = ⋃_{α<λ} V_α at limits

regularity ⇒ every set has a least rank α with x ∈ V_{α+1}.
the universe V = ⋃_α V_α is a proper class, not a set.`;
  }
  slider.addEventListener('input', render);
  render();
})();
</script>
</section>

2. Von Neumann ordinals

An ordinal is a transitive set well-ordered by $\in$. Concretely, von Neumann's recipe is:

\[ 0 = \emptyset,\qquad \alpha + 1 = \alpha \cup \{\alpha\},\qquad \lambda = \bigcup_{\beta < \lambda} \beta \text{ at limits}. \]

So $\alpha = \{\beta : \beta < \alpha\}$ — every ordinal is the set of smaller ordinals. The first few:

\[ 0=\emptyset,\quad 1=\{\emptyset\},\quad 2=\{0,1\},\quad 3=\{0,1,2\},\quad \dots,\quad \omega = \{0,1,2,\dots\},\quad \omega+1=\omega\cup\{\omega\}. \]
Why this encoding? Three things fall out for free. (i) $\alpha < \beta \iff \alpha \in \beta \iff \alpha \subsetneq \beta$ — order, membership, and subset coincide. (ii) The "next ordinal" is just $\alpha\cup\{\alpha\}$, no new machinery needed. (iii) $|\alpha|$ for finite $\alpha$ literally equals the integer $\alpha$.

The widget below draws the picket-fence picture: each ordinal is a height, each $\in$-arrow runs from a smaller to a larger one. Toggle between $0\dots 5$, $\omega$, $\omega+\omega$, and $\omega^2$:

Ordinal picket fence
click a button for the order type

Transfinite induction and recursion

Once $\in$ well-orders the ordinals, you get two principles for free:

Transfinite induction. If $\varphi(\alpha)$ holds whenever $\varphi(\beta)$ holds for all $\beta<\alpha$, then $\varphi(\alpha)$ holds for every ordinal $\alpha$.
Transfinite recursion. Given a class function $G$, there is a unique class function $F$ with $F(\alpha) = G(F\!\restriction\!\alpha)$. This is how $V_\alpha$, ordinal arithmetic, and Gödel's $L$ are all defined.

Induction lets you prove things layer by layer, even past $\omega$. Recursion lets you build things layer by layer; the price is replacement, which is what guarantees the recursion ranges over a set at each stage.

Used in
ZFC and ordinals · Cardinals and cofinality
ZFC and ordinals · Ordinal arithmetic

(function(){
  const buttons = $$('#w-ordpic button[data-mode]');
  const svg = $('#ordpic-svg'), out = $('#ordpic-out');
  let mode = 'finite';
  function render(){
    svg.innerHTML='';
    const W=620, H=220;

    } else if(mode==='omega2'){
      title = 'ω + ω = ω·2 — two ω-towers stacked';

      pickets.push({x: 270, h: 80, label: 'ω', color: 'var(--yellow)', limit: true});
      for(let i=0;i<10;i++) pickets.push({x: 295 + i*22, h: 10 + i*6, label: i<3?'ω+'+i:(i===9?'':''), color: 'var(--cyan)', mini:true});
      pickets.push({x: 535, h: 130, label: 'ω·2', color: 'var(--yellow)', limit: true});
    } else if(mode==='omegasq'){
      title = 'ω² = sup{ω·n : n<ω} — countably many ω-towers';
      const nGroups = 7;
      const gw = 70;
      for(let g=0;g<nGroups;g++){
        const baseX = 30 + g*gw;
        for(let i=0;i<5;i++) pickets.push({x: baseX + i*8, h: 6 + i*4, color: g%2? 'var(--cyan)':'var(--blue)', mini:true});
        pickets.push({x: baseX + 50, h: 50 + g*4, label: 'ω·'+(g+1), color: 'var(--yellow)', limit:true, small:true});
      }
      pickets.push({x: 555, h: 150, label: 'ω²', color: 'var(--violet)', limit:true});
    }
    // baseline
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:20,y1:baseY,x2:600,y2:baseY,stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-width':1}));
    // pickets
    for(const p of pickets){
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:p.x,y1:baseY,x2:p.x,y2:baseY-p.h,
        stroke:p.color,'stroke-width': p.limit? 2.4 : (p.mini? 1 : 1.6),
        'stroke-dasharray': p.limit? '4 3' : null}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:p.x,cy:baseY-p.h,r: p.limit? 4: (p.mini?1.6:2.6),
        fill:p.color}));
      if(p.label){

        t.textContent = p.label;
        svg.appendChild(t);

    }

    const ttext = SVG('text',{x:20, y:24, 'font-size':12, fill:'var(--mute)'});
    ttext.textContent = title; svg.appendChild(ttext);

    // readout
    const facts = {
      finite: 'each ordinal n is the set {0,1,…,n−1}.\nsuccessor: n+1 = n ∪ {n}.\nno limits below ω.',
      omega:  'ω = {0,1,2,…} is the smallest limit ordinal.\nit has no maximum element.\ncf(ω) = ω.\n|ω| = ℵ₀.',
      omega2: 'ω·2 = ω + ω; the second ω-tower lives "above" the first.\nω·2 ≠ 2·ω = ω.\ncf(ω·2) = ω.\n|ω·2| = ℵ₀.',
      omegasq:'ω² = ω·ω = sup{ω·n : n<ω} — countably many ω-towers.\ncf(ω²) = ω, still countable.\nthe Cantor normal form lives here: any α < ε₀ is a finite sum of ω^β·n terms.'
    };
    out.textContent = facts[mode];
  }
  buttons.forEach(b=>b.addEventListener('click',()=>{
    buttons.forEach(x=>x.classList.remove('active'));
    b.classList.add('active');
    mode = b.dataset.mode;
    render();
  }));
  render();
})();
</script>
</section>

3. Ordinal arithmetic

Sum, product, and exponentiation of ordinals are defined by transfinite recursion on the second argument:

\[ \alpha + 0 = \alpha,\quad \alpha + (\beta+1) = (\alpha+\beta)+1,\quad \alpha+\lambda = \sup_{\beta<\lambda}(\alpha+\beta). \] \[ \alpha\cdot 0 = 0,\quad \alpha\cdot(\beta+1) = \alpha\cdot\beta + \alpha,\quad \alpha\cdot\lambda = \sup_{\beta<\lambda}\alpha\cdot\beta. \] \[ \alpha^0 = 1,\quad \alpha^{\beta+1} = \alpha^\beta\cdot\alpha,\quad \alpha^\lambda = \sup_{\beta<\lambda}\alpha^\beta. \]
Warning — non-commutativity. Both addition and multiplication of ordinals fail to commute: $1 + \omega = \omega \ne \omega + 1$, and $2\cdot\omega = \omega \ne \omega\cdot 2$. Think of $\alpha+\beta$ as "place a copy of $\beta$ after $\alpha$": adding a single point to the front of $\omega$ doesn't change its order type, but adding one to the back makes a new top element.

Try the calculator. It runs the recursion on the second argument and shows the Cantor normal form:

Ordinal calculator
left and right operands of $\omega$-arithmetic

The tower up to $\varepsilon_0$

Every ordinal $\alpha < \varepsilon_0$ has a unique Cantor normal form \[ \alpha = \omega^{\beta_1}\cdot n_1 + \omega^{\beta_2}\cdot n_2 + \cdots + \omega^{\beta_k}\cdot n_k \] with $\beta_1>\beta_2>\cdots>\beta_k$ and $n_i\in\mathbb N_{>0}$. The first ordinal not of this form is $\varepsilon_0 = \sup\{\omega,\omega^\omega,\omega^{\omega^\omega},\dots\}$ — the least fixed point of $\alpha\mapsto\omega^\alpha$. Despite the towering iterated exponentials, $\varepsilon_0$ is still countable; the smallest uncountable ordinal $\omega_1$ is wildly larger.

Gentzen's theorem. Peano arithmetic cannot prove that $\varepsilon_0$ is well-founded. Adding well-foundedness of $\varepsilon_0$ as an extra principle (Gentzen, 1936) yields a consistency proof of PA. So $\varepsilon_0$ is, quantitatively, where the strength of PA runs out.
</section>

4. Cardinals and cofinality

Two sets have the same cardinality if there is a bijection between them. The cardinal $|X|$ is, in ZFC, the least ordinal in bijection with $X$ (this uses the well-ordering theorem, hence AC). For finite $X$ this is just the integer $|X|$; for infinite $X$ the cardinals are exactly the initial ordinals — those not in bijection with any smaller ordinal.

The aleph hierarchy. \[ \aleph_0 = \omega = |\mathbb N|,\qquad \aleph_{\alpha+1} = (\aleph_\alpha)^+ = \text{least cardinal} > \aleph_\alpha,\qquad \aleph_\lambda = \sup_{\beta<\lambda}\aleph_\beta. \] Every infinite cardinal is some $\aleph_\alpha$. The ordinal index $\alpha$ ranges over all ordinals — the alephs form a proper class.

Watch the ladder grow:

Aleph ladder
heights are schematic; each rung is uncountable beyond the next

Cofinality

The cofinality $\operatorname{cf}(\alpha)$ of a limit ordinal $\alpha$ is the least order type of an unbounded sequence in $\alpha$:

\[ \operatorname{cf}(\alpha) = \min\{\,|\,S\,| : S \subseteq \alpha,\ \sup S = \alpha\,\}. \]

A cardinal $\kappa$ is regular if $\operatorname{cf}(\kappa)=\kappa$, otherwise singular. In ZFC, every successor cardinal $\aleph_{\alpha+1}$ is regular. Limit cardinals can be singular: $\operatorname{cf}(\aleph_\omega)=\omega=\aleph_0$, because $\aleph_\omega = \sup_n\aleph_n$ is reached by a countable sequence.

König's theorem. $\kappa^{\operatorname{cf}(\kappa)} > \kappa$, so e.g. $2^{\aleph_0}\ne\aleph_\omega$ — the continuum cannot have countable cofinality, eliminating one popular guess for its size.

The continuum

$|\mathbb R|=2^{\aleph_0}$, the cardinality of the continuum. Two-way embedding: $\mathbb R \hookrightarrow \mathcal P(\mathbb Q)$ via Dedekind cuts $r\mapsto\{q\in\mathbb Q:q

5. The axiom of choice

Often phrased: for every family of nonempty sets $\{A_i\}_{i\in I}$, the product $\prod_i A_i$ is nonempty. Equivalently, there is a choice function $f\colon I\to\bigcup A_i$ with $f(i)\in A_i$ for all $i$.

Equivalent formulations (all over ZF):
  • Zorn's lemma. Every nonempty poset in which every chain has an upper bound has a maximal element.
  • Well-ordering theorem. Every set can be well-ordered.
  • Tychonoff. Any product of compact topological spaces is compact.
  • Vector space basis. Every vector space over a field has a basis.
  • Hahn–Banach. Bounded linear functionals on subspaces extend to the whole space (this is strictly weaker than full AC).

For finite or countable index sets you don't need AC — pick by induction. The full strength shows up for uncountable products. Solovay (1970) showed that ZF + DC (dependent choice) is consistent with "every set of reals is Lebesgue measurable", so any non-measurable set (Vitali, Banach–Tarski) genuinely requires more than DC.

Choice function picker
click into each bin to pick a representative

See also

<script>
(function(){
  const svg=$('#ch-svg'), out=$('#ch-out');
  const clearBtn=$('#ch-clear'), randBtn=$('#ch-rand');
  // 5 bins, each with 3 elements; pick[i] ∈ {-1,0,1,2}
  const N=5, K=3;
  let pick = Array(N).fill(-1);

  function render(){
    svg.innerHTML='';
    for(let i=0;i<N;i++){
      const bx = 40 + i*112, by = 30, bw = 90, bh = 140;
      svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:bx, y:by, width:bw, height:bh, rx:10, ry:10,
        fill:'rgba(154,164,178,0.06)', stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-dasharray':'4 4'}));
      const t = SVG('text',{x:bx+bw/2, y:by-6,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':12, fill:'var(--mute)'});
      t.textContent = `A_${i+1}`;
      svg.appendChild(t);
      for(let j=0;j<K;j++){
        const cx = bx + bw/2, cy = by + 30 + j*36;
        const chosen = pick[i]===j;
        svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx, cy, r:14,
          fill: chosen ? 'var(--yellow)' : 'var(--panel2)',
          stroke: chosen ? 'var(--yellow)' : 'var(--ink)',
          'stroke-width': chosen ? 2 : 1,
          style:'cursor:pointer',
          'data-bin': String(i), 'data-elt': String(j)}));
        const lbl = SVG('text',{x:cx, y:cy+4,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':12,
          fill: chosen ? '#0b0d10' : 'var(--ink)','font-weight':700,
          'pointer-events':'none'});
        lbl.textContent = `${i+1}${'abc'[j]}`;
        svg.appendChild(lbl);
      }
    }
    const allPicked = pick.every(p=>p>=0);
    const status = allPicked ?
`✓ choice function f defined:
  f(${[1,2,3,4,5].map((i,k)=>`A_${i}`).join(', ')}) = (${pick.map((p,i)=>`${i+1}${'abc'[p]}`).join(', ')})

with finitely many bins this is trivial — no AC needed.` :
`pick one element from each of ${N} bins to define f: {1,…,${N}} → ⋃A_i.

picked so far: ${pick.filter(p=>p>=0).length}/${N}.

— for FINITE families this is ZF-provable by induction.
— for INFINITE families, the existence of f is exactly the axiom of choice.`;
    out.textContent = status;
  }
  svg.addEventListener('click',(e)=>{
    const bin = e.target.getAttribute && e.target.getAttribute('data-bin');
    const elt = e.target.getAttribute && e.target.getAttribute('data-elt');
    if(bin===null||bin===undefined||elt===null||elt===undefined) return;
    pick[parseInt(bin,10)] = parseInt(elt,10);
    render();
  });
  clearBtn.addEventListener('click',()=>{ pick = Array(N).fill(-1); render(); });
  randBtn.addEventListener('click',()=>{ pick = pick.map(()=>Math.floor(Math.random()*K)); render(); });
  render();
})();
</script>
</section>

6. The continuum hypothesis

Cantor proved $|\mathbb N| < |\mathbb R|$ in 1874, and conjectured the continuum hypothesis:

\[ \text{(CH)}\qquad 2^{\aleph_0} = \aleph_1. \]

That is: there is no cardinal strictly between $\aleph_0$ and $|\mathbb R|$. He could not prove it. Hilbert listed it as Problem 1 in 1900. The resolution came in two halves:

yearwhowhat
1940Gödelbuilds the constructible universe $L \subseteq V$, an inner model of ZFC where CH (and GCH) hold. So ZFC + CH is consistent (assuming ZFC is).
1963Coheninvents forcing; constructs a model of ZFC where $2^{\aleph_0}=\aleph_2$. So ZFC + ¬CH is consistent (assuming ZFC is).
Independence. CH is neither provable nor refutable from ZFC. The axioms simply do not pin the size of the continuum down beyond König's $\operatorname{cf}(2^{\aleph_0})>\omega$. Modern research (Woodin, Foreman, …) studies which extensions of ZFC settle CH and how.

Where is $2^{\aleph_0}$?
click candidates

Used in
Forcing and independence · Independence of the continuum hypothesis

<script>
(function(){
  const svg=$('#ch-svg2'), out=$('#ch-out2');
  // candidates: ℵ₀, ℵ₁, ℵ₂, ℵ_ω, ℵ_{ω+1}
  const cand = [
    {x:80,  label:'ℵ₀',    note:'No — Cantor 1874: |ℝ|>|ℕ|.', ok:false},
    {x:200, label:'ℵ₁',    note:'CH says yes (Gödel: consistent with ZFC).', ok:true},
    {x:320, label:'ℵ₂',    note:'¬CH at this value (Cohen: also consistent with ZFC).', ok:true},
    {x:440, label:'ℵ_ω',   note:'No — König forbids this; cf(2^ℵ₀) > ω.', ok:false},
    {x:540, label:'ℵ_{ω+1}', note:'Possible — successor cardinals are regular.', ok:true},
  ];
  let chosen = -1;
  function render(){
    svg.innerHTML='';
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:20,y1:120,x2:600,y2:120,stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-width':1}));
    for(let i=0;i<cand.length;i++){
      const c = cand[i];
      const fill = (chosen===i) ? (c.ok?'var(--green)':'var(--pink)') : 'var(--panel2)';
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:c.x, cy:120, r:24, fill,
        stroke: c.ok?'var(--green)':'var(--pink)','stroke-width':2,
        style:'cursor:pointer','data-i':String(i)}));
      const t = SVG('text',{x:c.x, y:125,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':13,
        fill: chosen===i? '#0b0d10':'var(--ink)','font-weight':700,'pointer-events':'none'});
      t.textContent = c.label; svg.appendChild(t);
    }
    // headline
    const h = SVG('text',{x:310, y:36,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':14, fill:'var(--mute)'});
    h.textContent = '2^ℵ₀  =  ?'; svg.appendChild(h);
    // result text
    if(chosen<0) out.textContent = 'click a candidate.';
    else {
      const c = cand[chosen];
      out.textContent =
`candidate: 2^ℵ₀ = ${c.label}
verdict:   ${c.ok?'CONSISTENT with ZFC':'IMPOSSIBLE in ZFC'}
why:       ${c.note}

ZFC restricts 2^ℵ₀ only by:  cf(2^ℵ₀) > ω  (König).
within that constraint, every aleph value is consistent with ZFC.`;
    }
  }
  svg.addEventListener('click',(e)=>{
    const i = e.target.getAttribute && e.target.getAttribute('data-i');
    if(i===null||i===undefined) return;
    chosen = parseInt(i,10);
    render();
  });
  render();
})();
</script>
</section>

7. Inaccessible cardinals (and Grothendieck universes)

An uncountable cardinal $\kappa$ is (strongly) inaccessible if it is

  • regular: $\operatorname{cf}(\kappa)=\kappa$, and
  • a strong limit: $\lambda < \kappa \Rightarrow 2^\lambda < \kappa$.

The point: $V_\kappa$ is then closed under all the ZFC set-construction operations (pairing, union, power-set, replacement bounded by $\kappa$). So $V_\kappa$ itself is a model of ZFC.

Why this is "more than ZFC". If ZFC + (∃ inaccessible) proves Con(ZFC), then by Gödel's second incompleteness theorem ZFC alone cannot prove the existence of an inaccessible (else ZFC would prove its own consistency). Inaccessibles are the bottom rung of the large cardinal hierarchy; above them sit Mahlo, weakly compact, measurable, …, supercompact, Woodin, and Reinhardt.

Grothendieck universes

A Grothendieck universe $\mathcal U$ is a transitive set with

  • $x\in\mathcal U \Rightarrow \mathcal P(x)\in\mathcal U$,
  • $x\in\mathcal U,\ y\in x \Rightarrow y\in\mathcal U$ (transitive),
  • $\{x_i\}_{i\in I}$ a family with $I\in\mathcal U$ and each $x_i\in\mathcal U$ implies $\bigcup_i x_i \in\mathcal U$.

Theorem (Grothendieck): a set $\mathcal U$ with these closure properties is exactly $V_\kappa$ for $\kappa$ an inaccessible cardinal (in the uncountable case). The "universe axiom" — every set lies in some Grothendieck universe — is what SGA assumes to make category-theoretic constructions like $\mathbf{Set}$, $\mathbf{Cat}$, sheaf categories, derived functors all live coherently as actual sets rather than proper classes. It is equivalent to the existence of a proper class of inaccessibles.

Practical translation. When a category theorist says "let $\mathcal U$ be a universe and consider $\mathcal U$-small categories", they are implicitly assuming (at least) one inaccessible cardinal beyond ZFC. Most working mathematicians never notice; the few who care are usually doing higher-categorical foundations or set-theoretic universes.

Large cardinal tower
consistency strength climbs left → right

<script>
(function(){
  const svg=$('#lc-svg'), out=$('#lc-out');
  const tiers = [
    {x: 60, h: 30,  label:'ZFC',          desc:'baseline'},
    {x:150, h: 60,  label:'inaccessible', desc:'V_κ ⊨ ZFC'},
    {x:230, h: 80,  label:'Mahlo',        desc:'inaccessibles below it form a stationary set'},
    {x:320, h:100,  label:'weakly compact',desc:'tree property'},
    {x:410, h:120,  label:'measurable',   desc:'admits a κ-complete ultrafilter'},
    {x:500, h:140,  label:'Woodin',       desc:'projective determinacy'},
    {x:580, h:160,  label:'supercompact', desc:'closed under j-elementary embeddings'},
  ];
  function render(){
    svg.innerHTML='';
    const baseY=170;
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:20,y1:baseY,x2:600,y2:baseY,stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    for(const t of tiers){
      svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:t.x-26,y:baseY-t.h,width:52,height:t.h,
        fill:`color-mix(in srgb, var(--violet) ${10 + (t.h/2)|0}%, transparent)`,
        stroke:'var(--violet)','stroke-width':1.5}));
      const tx = SVG('text',{x:t.x, y:baseY-t.h-6,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':10,
        fill:'var(--violet)','font-weight':600});
      tx.textContent = t.label; svg.appendChild(tx);
    }
    out.textContent =
`each rung's existence implies consistency of all rungs to its left.
inaccessible    — V_κ ⊨ ZFC; strictly past plain ZFC by Gödel II.
mahlo           — its inaccessibles are stationary in κ.
weakly compact  — combinatorially rich; tree property.
measurable      — Scott (1961): incompatible with V = L.
Woodin / supercompact — modern set theory; force determinacy axioms in inner models.

(Reinhardt cardinals were shown inconsistent with ZFC by Kunen, 1971.)`;
  }
  render();
})();
</script>
</section>
